```text
1	---
2	type: task
3	status: backlog
4	priority: 2
5	created: 2026-03-31
6	---
7	
8	# Fix Cloudflare beacon CORS error
9	
10	Cross-Origin Request Blocked for `https://static.cloudflareinsights.com/beacon.min.js`. The Cloudflare Web Analytics script is failing to load due to CORS policy.
11	
12	## Context
13	
14	This is injected by Cloudflare (Web Analytics), not by project code. Reproduces in production at https://nasapicture.com/. Likely caused by a Content-Security-Policy header or Cloudflare Web Analytics configuration issue. Investigate whether CSP headers need adjusting or the analytics snippet needs updating in the Cloudflare dashboard.
15
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	---
2	type: task
3	status: backlog
4	priority: 2
5	created: 2026-03-31
6	---
7	
8	# Fix Cloudflare beacon SRI hash mismatch
9	
10	The `integrity` attribute (sha512) on the Cloudflare Web Analytics beacon script does not match the content of the fetched resource. The computed hash is a zero-byte hash, suggesting the resource failed to load (likely related to the CORS error in TASK-0005) and the browser is comparing against an empty response.
11	
12	## Context
13	
14	Reproduces in production at https://nasapicture.com/. This is a downstream symptom of the CORS failure — if TASK-0005 is resolved, this will likely resolve too. If not, the SRI hash in the injected snippet may be stale and need regeneration via the Cloudflare dashboard.
15
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	---
2	type: task
3	status: backlog
4	priority: 2
5	created: 2026-03-31
6	---
7	
8	# Fix empty datetime attribute on time element
9	
10	The `<time datetime="">` element in the landing page HTML (line 477) has an empty `datetime` attribute. The value is populated by JS after load, but the initial server-rendered HTML is invalid per the W3C validator — `datetime` must match a valid time-datetime format if present.
11	
12	## Fix options
13	
14	- Remove the `datetime` attribute from the initial HTML and only add it via JS when the value is available
15	- Pre-populate with a fallback date server-side
16
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
8	# Fix hero section missing heading

11	
12	## Fix options
13	
14	- Add a visually-hidden heading (e.g., `<h2 class="sr-only">Today's Astronomy Picture</h2>`)
15	- Change the `<section>` to a `<div>` if it doesn't represent a distinct document section
```

```text
1	/**
2	 * APOD Proxy — Cloudflare Worker
3	 *
4	 * Serves NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day from a stable URL.
5	 * Uses Cloudflare's edge cache for both the API response and the
6	 * image bytes — no KV, no cron, no external storage.
7	 *
8	 * Routes:
9	 *   GET /      → today's APOD image (high res, falls back to standard)
10	 *   GET /sd    → today's APOD image (standard res)
11	 *   GET /info  → JSON metadata
12	 *   GET /about → plain-text description and attribution
13	 */
14	
15	export interface Env {
16	  NASA_API_KEY?: string;
17	}
18	
19	const NASA_APOD_URL = "https://api.nasa.gov/planetary/apod";
20	const MAX_LOOKBACK_DAYS = 7;
21	const CACHE_TTL = 3600;
22	
23	interface ApodResponse {
24	  date: string;
25	  title: string;
26	  explanation: string;
27	  url: string;
28	  hdurl?: string;
29	  media_type: "image" | "video";
30	  copyright?: string;
31	}
32	
33	class NasaApiError extends Error {
34	  constructor(public statusCode: number, message: string) {
35	    super(message);
36	  }
37	}
38	
39	function apodCacheUrl(key: string): string {
40	  return `http://apod-cache/${key}`;
41	}
42	
43	async function fetchApod(log: string[], apiKey: string, date?: string): Promise<ApodResponse> {
44	  const cacheKey = date ?? "today";
45	
46	  const cache = caches.default;
47	  const cacheReq = new Request(apodCacheUrl(cacheKey));
48	  const cached = await cache.match(cacheReq);
49	  if (cached) {
50	    log.push(`  [fetchApod] ${cacheKey} → cache HIT`);
51	    return cached.json() as Promise<ApodResponse>;
52	  }
53	
54	  const url = date
55	    ? `${NASA_APOD_URL}?api_key=${apiKey}&date=${date}`
56	    : `${NASA_APOD_URL}?api_key=${apiKey}`;
57	
58	  const res = await fetch(url);
59	
60	  const cfCache = res.headers.get("cf-cache-status") ?? "none";
61	  log.push(`  [fetchApod] ${cacheKey} → ${res.status} (cf-cache: ${cfCache})`);
62	
63	  if (!res.ok) {
64	    const body = await res.text();
65	    log.push(`  [fetchApod] body=${body}`);
66	    throw new NasaApiError(res.status,
67	      res.status === 429
68	        ? "NASA API rate limit exceeded — try again later"
69	        : `NASA API returned ${res.status}`
70	    );
71	  }
72	
73	  const apod = await res.json() as ApodResponse;
74	  await cache.put(cacheReq, new Response(JSON.stringify(apod), {
75	    headers: { "Cache-Control": `public, max-age=${CACHE_TTL}` },
76	  }));
77	  return apod;
78	}
79	
80	async function findLatestImage(log: string[], apiKey: string): Promise<ApodResponse> {
81	  let videoStreak = 0;
82	
83	  for (let i = 0; i <= MAX_LOOKBACK_DAYS; i++) {
84	    const date = i === 0
85	      ? undefined
86	      : (() => { const d = new Date(); d.setDate(d.getDate() - i); return d.toLocaleDateString("en-CA", { timeZone: "America/New_York" }); })();
87	
88	    const apod = await fetchApod(log, apiKey, date);
89	    if (apod.media_type === "image") return apod;
90	    videoStreak++;
91	  }
92	
93	  throw new Error(
94	    `Today's APOD is a video, and the last ${videoStreak} days have been too — no image available`
95	  );
96	}
97	
98	async function fetchImageCached(log: string[], url: string): Promise<Response> {
99	  const cache = caches.default;
100	  const cacheReq = new Request(url);
101	  const cached = await cache.match(cacheReq);
102	  if (cached) {
103	    log.push(`  [fetchImage] ${url.slice(0, 80)}… → cache HIT`);
104	    return cached;
105	  }
106	
107	  const res = await fetch(url);
108	
109	  log.push(`  [fetchImage] ${url.slice(0, 80)}… → ${res.status}`);
110	
111	  if (!res.ok) {
112	    throw new Error(`Image fetch failed: ${res.status}`);
113	  }
114	
115	  const cloned = res.clone();
116	  await cache.put(cacheReq, new Response(cloned.body, {
117	    headers: { ...Object.fromEntries(res.headers), "Cache-Control": `public, max-age=${CACHE_TTL}` },
118	  }));
119	  return res;
120	}
121	
122	function baseHeaders(origin: string, headers: Record<string, string> = {}): Record<string, string> {
123	  return {
124	    "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "*",
125	    "Cache-Control": `public, max-age=${CACHE_TTL}`,
126	    "Link": [
127	      `<${origin}/>; rel="alternate"; type="image/*"; title="HD image"`,
128	      `<${origin}/sd>; rel="alternate"; type="image/*"; title="SD image"`,
129	      `<${origin}/info>; rel="describedby"`,
130	      `<${origin}/about>; rel="about"`,
131	    ].join(", "),
132	    ...headers,
133	  };
134	}
135	
136	export default {
137	  async fetch(request: Request, env: Env): Promise<Response> {
138	    if (request.method === "OPTIONS") {
139	      return new Response(null, {
140	        headers: {
141	          "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "*",
142	          "Access-Control-Allow-Methods": "GET, OPTIONS",
143	          "Access-Control-Max-Age": "86400",
144	        },
145	      });
146	    }
147	
148	    const reqUrl = new URL(request.url);
149	    const { pathname } = reqUrl;
150	    const origin = reqUrl.origin;
151	    const start = Date.now();
152	
153	    const log: string[] = [];
154	    let res: Response;
155	
156	    try {
157	      const apiKey = env.NASA_API_KEY || "DEMO_KEY";
158	
159	      const sdPaths = ["/sd", "/image-sd.jpg"];
160	      const hdPaths = ["/", "/image.jpg"];
161	
162	      if (hdPaths.includes(pathname) || sdPaths.includes(pathname)) {
163	        const apod = await findLatestImage(log, apiKey);
164	        const imageUrl = sdPaths.includes(pathname)
165	          ? apod.url
166	          : (apod.hdurl || apod.url);
167	
168	        const upstream = await fetchImageCached(log, imageUrl);
169	        const contentType = upstream.headers.get("Content-Type") || "image/jpeg";
170	
171	        res = new Response(upstream.body, {
172	          headers: baseHeaders(origin, { "Content-Type": contentType }),
173	        });
174	      } else if (pathname === "/info") {
175	        const endpoints = {
176	          "/": "Today's APOD image (high resolution, falls back to standard)",
177	          "/image.jpg": "Same as / with file extension (for contexts that need one)",
178	          "/sd": "Today's APOD image (standard resolution)",
179	          "/image-sd.jpg": "Same as /sd with file extension",
180	          "/info": "JSON metadata for today's APOD",
181	          "/about": "Plain-text description and attribution",
182	        };
183	
184	        try {
185	          const apod = await fetchApod(log, apiKey);
186	
187	          res = new Response(
188	            JSON.stringify({
189	              title: apod.title,
190	              date: apod.date,
191	              explanation: apod.explanation,
192	              media_type: apod.media_type,
193	              url: apod.url,
194	              hdurl: apod.hdurl ?? null,
195	              copyright: apod.copyright ?? null,
196	              endpoints,
197	            }, null, 2),
198	            { headers: baseHeaders(origin, { "Content-Type": "application/json" }) },
199	          );
200	        } catch (err) {
201	          const message = err instanceof Error ? err.message : "Unknown error";
202	          const status = err instanceof NasaApiError && err.statusCode === 429 ? 429 : 502;
203	          res = new Response(
204	            JSON.stringify({ error: message, endpoints }, null, 2),
205	            { status, headers: baseHeaders(origin, { "Content-Type": "application/json" }) },
206	          );
207	        }
208	      } else if (pathname === "/about") {
209	        const text = [
210	          "",
211	          "     .        *          .          *          .        *",
212	          "   .         *                        .          .",
213	          "                 _   _    _    ____    _",
214	          "     *          | \\ | |  / \\  / ___|  / \\         .",
215	          "        .       |  \\| | / _ \\ \\___ \\ / _ \\",
216	          "           .    | |\\  |/ ___ \\ ___) / ___ \\   *",
217	          "     .          |_| \\_/_/   \\_\\____/_/   \\_\\        .",
218	          "  *                              .                *",
219	          "        .    *        .    *          .        .",
220	          "",
221	          "                   ── APOD Proxy ──",
222	          "",
223	          "  Proxy for NASA's Astronomy Picture of the Day.",
224	          "  Returns today's image at a static URL, no API key required.",
225	          "  Video days are skipped automatically.",
226	          "  Useful for wallpapers, <img> sources, and other zero-JS environments.",
227	          "",
228	          "  Usage:",
229	          `    <img src="${origin}/">`,
230	          "",
231	          "  Routes:",
232	          "    /             HD image (default)",
233	          "    /image.jpg    HD image (with extension)",
234	          "    /sd           Standard resolution",
235	          "    /image-sd.jpg Standard resolution (with extension)",
236	          "    /info         JSON metadata",
237	          "    /about        This page",
238	          "",
239	          "  Homepage: https://nasapicture.com",
240	          "",
241	          "  Built by Grant Keller | https://grantkeller.dev",
242	          "",
243	        ].join("\n");
244	
245	        res = new Response(text, {
246	          headers: baseHeaders(origin, { "Content-Type": "text/plain; charset=utf-8" }),
247	        });
248	      } else {
249	        res = new Response(
250	          JSON.stringify({ error: "Not found. Routes: /, /sd, /info, /about" }),
251	          { status: 404, headers: baseHeaders(origin, { "Content-Type": "application/json" }) },
252	        );
253	      }
254	    } catch (err) {
255	      const message = err instanceof Error ? err.message : "Unknown error";
256	      const status = err instanceof NasaApiError && err.statusCode === 429 ? 429 : 502;
257	      res = new Response(
258	        JSON.stringify({ error: message }),
259	        { status, headers: baseHeaders(origin, { "Content-Type": "application/json" }) },
260	      );
261	    }
262	
263	    console.log(`────────────────────────────────────────`);
264	    console.log(`${request.method} ${pathname} → ${res.status} (${Date.now() - start}ms)`);
265	    for (const line of log) console.log(line);
266	    return res;
267	  },
268	};
269
```

```text
455	<body>
456	  <header class="site-header">
457	    <a href="/" class="logo">NASApicture<span class="logo-dot">.</span>com</a>
458	    <h1 class="hero-tagline">Today's Astronomy Picture of the Day</h1>
459	    <nav class="site-nav" aria-label="Site navigation">
460	      <a href="https://api.nasapicture.com/about" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">/about</a>
461	    </nav>
462	  </header>
463	
464	  <main>
465	  <section class="hero">
466	    <figure class="hero-image-area">
467	      <img
468	        src="https://api.nasapicture.com/"
469	        alt="NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day"
470	        class="hero-img"
471	        id="hero-img"
472	        loading="eager"
473	        fetchpriority="high"
474	      >
475	      <figcaption class="hero-caption" id="hero-caption">
476	        <p class="hero-caption-title" id="caption-title"></p>
477	        <time class="hero-caption-date" id="caption-date" datetime=""></time>
478	      </figcaption>
479	    </figure>
480	
481	    <div class="usage-row">
482	      <p class="usage-item"><span>New day, new photo, same URL</span> <code class="usage-url">https://api.nasapicture.com<button type="button" class="copy-btn" data-copy="https://api.nasapicture.com/" aria-label="Copy URL"><svg class="copy" aria-hidden="true" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2"/><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h9a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v1"/></svg><svg class="check" aria-hidden="true" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"/></svg></button></code></p>
483	      <p class="usage-item"><span>Use it anywhere you'd put an image URL</span> <code class="usage-url">&lt;img src="https://api.nasapicture.com/"&gt;<button type="button" class="copy-btn" data-copy='&lt;img src="https://api.nasapicture.com/"&gt;' aria-label="Copy img tag"><svg class="copy" aria-hidden="true" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2"/><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h9a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v1"/></svg><svg class="check" aria-hidden="true" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"/></svg></button></code></p>
484	    </div>
485	  </section>
486	
487	  <section class="info">
488	    <div class="fade-up">
489	      <h2>Endpoints</h2>
490	      <nav class="endpoint-list" aria-label="API endpoints">
491	        <a class="endpoint-row" href="https://api.nasapicture.com/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
492	          <span class="endpoint-route">/</span>
493	          <span class="endpoint-desc">HD image, falls back to standard</span>
494	          <span class="endpoint-type">image/*</span>
495	        </a>
496	        <a class="endpoint-row" href="https://api.nasapicture.com/sd" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
497	          <span class="endpoint-route">/sd</span>
498	          <span class="endpoint-desc">Standard resolution image</span>
499	          <span class="endpoint-type">image/*</span>
500	        </a>
501	        <a class="endpoint-row" href="https://api.nasapicture.com/info" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
502	          <span class="endpoint-route">/info</span>
503	          <span class="endpoint-desc">Title, date, explanation, URLs, copyright</span>
504	          <span class="endpoint-type">application/json</span>
505	        </a>
506	        <a class="endpoint-row" href="https://api.nasapicture.com/about" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
507	          <span class="endpoint-route">/about</span>
508	          <span class="endpoint-desc">Description and route listing</span>
509	          <span class="endpoint-type">text/plain</span>
510	        </a>
511	      </nav>
512	    </div>
513	  </section>
514	  </main>
515	
516	  <footer>
517	    <div class="footer-inner">
518	      <p>Built by <a href="https://grantkeller.dev" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Grant Keller</a></p>
519	      <p>
520	        <a href="https://apod.nasa.gov/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">NASA APOD</a>
521	        <span class="footer-sep" aria-hidden="true">/</span>
522	        <a href="https://github.com/GDKeller/nasa-picture-proxy" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Source</a>
523	      </p>
524	    </div>
525	  </footer>
526	
527	  <script>
528	    const PROD_BASE = 'https://api.nasapicture.com';
529	    const API_BASE = location.hostname === 'localhost' ? `http://localhost:8787` : PROD_BASE;
530	    const img = document.getElementById('hero-img');
531	
532	    function onImageReady() { img.classList.add('loaded'); }
533	
534	    // Rewrite all production URLs to use API_BASE
535	    const defined_replace = (str) => str.replace(PROD_BASE, API_BASE);
536	    img.src = defined_replace(img.src);
537	    document.querySelectorAll(`a[href^="${PROD_BASE}"]`).forEach(a => {
538	      a.href = defined_replace(a.href);
539	    });
540	    document.querySelectorAll('.copy-btn[data-copy]').forEach(btn => {
541	      btn.dataset.copy = defined_replace(btn.dataset.copy);
542	    });
543	    document.querySelectorAll('.usage-url').forEach(el => {
544	      el.childNodes.forEach(node => {
545	        if (node.nodeType === Node.TEXT_NODE) {
546	          node.textContent = defined_replace(node.textContent);
547	        }
548	      });
549	    });
550	
551	    // Fetch APOD metadata for caption (image loads directly from proxy)
552	    fetch(`${API_BASE}/info`)
553	      .then(r => r.json())
554	      .then(data => {
555	        if (data.title) {
556	          img.alt = data.title;
557	          document.getElementById('caption-title').textContent = data.title;
558	          const dateEl = document.getElementById('caption-date');
559	          dateEl.textContent = data.date;
560	          dateEl.setAttribute('datetime', data.date);
561	          document.getElementById('hero-caption').classList.add('visible');
562	        }
563	      })
564	      .catch(() => {});
565	
566	    img.addEventListener('load', onImageReady);
567	
568	    // Copy buttons
569	    document.querySelectorAll('.copy-btn').forEach(btn => {
570	      btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
571	        const text = btn.dataset.copy;
572	        navigator.clipboard.writeText(text).then(() => {
573	          btn.classList.add('copied');
574	          setTimeout(() => btn.classList.remove('copied'), 2000);
575	        });
576	      });
577	    });
578	
579	    // Scroll fade-in
580	    const observer = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {
581	      entries.forEach(entry => {
582	        if (entry.isIntersecting) {
583	          entry.target.classList.add('visible');
584	          observer.unobserve(entry.target);
585	        }
586	      });
587	    }, { threshold: 0.15 });
588	
589	    document.querySelectorAll('.fade-up').forEach(el => observer.observe(el));
590	  </script>
591	</body>
592	</html>
593
```

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 01:52:05Z)**_

The CORS and SRI errors (TASK-0005/0006) are about the beacon script snippet that Cloudflare injects into your page. You won't find that config here on the **Analytics & logs > Web analytics** dashboard view.

Look for the actual **Web Analytics site configuration** — scroll down to the "Quick Actions" section visible at the bottom and click **"View Web Analytics sites"**. From there you should be able to see the JS snippet configuration for nasapicture.com. The fix is likely one of:

1. **Re-generating the snippet** — if the beacon script URL or SRI hash is stale
2. **Disabling and re-enabling Web Analytics** for the site to get a fresh injection

The CORS error suggests the beacon script isn't being served with the right `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header, which is entirely on Cloudflare's side. If re-generating doesn't fix it, it may be a known Cloudflare bug worth checking their status/community forums for.

None of the “sha512” hashes in the integrity attribute match the content of the subresource at “https://static.cloudflareinsights.com/beacon.min.js/v8c78df7c7c0f484497ecbca7046644da1771523124516”. The computed hash is “z4PhNX7vuL3xVChQ1m2AB9Yg5AULVxXcg/SpIdNs6c5H0NE8XYXysP+DGNKHfuwvY7kxvUdBeoGlODJ6+SfaPg==”.

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 04:18:01Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 04:18:05Z)**_

That SHA-512 hash is the hash of an empty string — confirming the CORS failure prevents the script from loading, so the browser hashes nothing and it doesn't match. This is a known Cloudflare issue, particularly in Firefox.

Since this is Cloudflare's infrastructure problem (their CDN not returning CORS headers for `static.cloudflareinsights.com`), your options are:

1. **Switch to "Enable with JS Snippet installation"** — manually embed the beacon in your landing page HTML *without* the `integrity` attribute, which sidesteps the SRI check
2. **Leave it** — this only affects analytics collection, not site functionality. Chrome/Safari may not exhibit the same issue

Want to try option 1, or just mark these tasks as won't-fix/external?

ahhh it appears this was bc i had "tracking protection" on in Zen

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 04:19:32Z)**_

That'll do it. Tracking protection blocks requests to known analytics domains like `static.cloudflareinsights.com`, which causes the CORS failure, which cascades into the SRI hash mismatch (hashing an empty response).

Both TASK-0005 and TASK-0006 are non-issues — the site works correctly for users without tracking protection, and the beacon failing gracefully for those with it is expected behavior.

Want me to close those tasks out?

Create a new item in the `board/` project management system. The system spec is bundled with the plugin at `/Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/spec/board-system-spec.md`.

Before creating, check if a similar item already exists. If a close match is found, ask the user if they still want to create a new one.

## Scope Assessment

When the user requests a **TASK**, evaluate whether it is actually EPIC-sized before creating it. Look for these signals:

- Multiple distinct steps or deliverables in the description
- Multiple action verbs joined by "and" (e.g., "add auth and set up roles and integrate SSO")
- Work that spans different code areas or concerns
- Sub-steps that could be completed and shipped independently

**Quick test:** "Could someone meaningfully complete and ship part of this while the rest remains undone?" If yes → likely an EPIC.

If EPIC signals are present, suggest an EPIC + sub-TASKs with a concrete breakdown. For example:

> "This looks like it has multiple independent deliverables. Want me to create an EPIC with separate TASKs for each piece instead?"

**This is a soft suggestion only.** If the user says "just make it a task," comply without pushback.

This assessment only applies to TASK creation — do not second-guess EPICs, NOTEs, or other types.

## Arguments

The user provides the type and title: `/board:board-add task <title>`, `/board:board-add epic <title>`, etc.

If no type is given, ask the user what type of item to create.

## Determine Next ID

Run the ID assignment script:

```bash
bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/next-id.sh <TYPE> ./board
```

This scans all directories (including `board/archive/` and `.doc-ids`) and returns the next available 4-digit zero-padded ID.

## Create the File

Use the correct directory, frontmatter, and naming convention:

| Type | Directory | Filename Example |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| task | `board/tasks/` | `TASK-0014_add-batch-processing.md` |
| epic | `board/planning/` | `EPIC-0009_batch-mode.md` |
| adr | `board/planning/` | `ADR-0002_chose-webgpu-over-wasm.md` |
| note | `board/planning/` | `NOTE-0001_explore-wasm-option.md` |
| reference | `board/reference/` | `REFE-0007_webgpu-overview.md` |
| resource | `board/resources/` | `RESO-0001_webgpu-article.md` |

## Frontmatter Templates

Task:
```yaml
---
type: task
status: backlog
priority: 2
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Epic:
```yaml
---
type: epic
status: planning
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

ADR:
```yaml
---
type: adr
status: active
context_date: YYYY-MM-DD
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Note:
```yaml
---
type: note
status: inbox
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Reference:
```yaml
---
type: reference
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

Resource (ask user for URL):
```yaml
---
type: resource
status: inbox
url: <url>
created: YYYY-MM-DD
---
```

## Naming Rules

- Convert title to kebab-case for filename
- Task titles MUST be imperative mood (start with a verb). If the user gives a noun phrase, rewrite it (e.g., "batch processing" -> "Add batch processing support")
- Use today's date for `created`

## After Creation

Show the file path and ask if the user wants to add more detail to the body.

```md
# Generate a daily OG image

Generate a new Open Graph image every day, resized and cropped to the optimal 1200x630px dimensions. Adding a text overlay with the logo would be a bonus.

## Context

The current OG image points to `https://api.nasapicture.com/image.jpg`, which serves the raw APOD at whatever dimensions NASA provides (often square or portrait). Social platforms expect 1200x630px for optimal previews.

## Approach ideas

- Cloudflare Image Transformations (resize/crop via URL params) — may handle this without custom code
- Cloudflare Worker with an image processing library to resize, crop, and composite the logo overlay
```

Research Cloudflare image options

```text
Here is a summary of the findings.

---

## Cloudflare Image Tools -- Research Summary

### 1. Cloudflare Image Resizing / Transformations

**What it does:** Resize, crop, rotate, adjust quality, blur, sharpen, and change format of images on the fly. Two ways to use it from a Worker:

- **`cf.image` on `fetch()` calls** -- pass options like `width`, `height`, `fit`, `gravity`, `quality`, `format`, `draw` (overlays) directly in the fetch options object.
- **Images binding (`env.IMAGES`)** -- a newer, more flexible API. You add `[images] binding = "IMAGES"` to `wrangler.toml`, then chain `.input()`, `.transform()`, `.draw()`, `.output()` calls. This works with raw streams (no public URL required), so you can pipe bytes from R2, KV, or another fetch.

**Pricing:**
- **Free plan: 5,000 unique transformations per month at no cost.** Beyond that, new transformations return a 9422 error (cached ones keep working).
- **Paid plan:** First 5,000 included, then **$0.50 per 1,000 unique transformations/month.** A "unique transformation" = one original image + one set of parameters. The same combo requested again in the same month is not re-billed. The `format=auto` parameter (serving AVIF vs WebP to different clients) counts as only one transformation.
- Important: with the Images binding, **every call counts as a transformation** regardless of uniqueness.

**Available on free plans?** Yes, with the 5,000/month cap. No credit card required to start.

### 2. Cloudflare Images (Storage Product) vs Image Resizing

These were merged into a single product in late 2023. The distinction now is:

| Feature | Transformations (free + paid) | Images Storage (paid only) |
|---|---|---|
| Transform remote images (your origin, R2, etc.) | Yes | N/A |
| Store images in CF's managed bucket | No | Yes ($5/100K stored/mo) |
| Serve stored images | N/A | Yes ($1/100K delivered/mo) |
| Predefined variants (thumbnail, hero, etc.) | N/A | Yes |

For your use case (proxying NASA APOD), you would only use the **Transformations** feature against a remote origin. You would not need the storage product.

### 3. Image Compositing (Text Overlay, Logo Overlay)

**Image-over-image overlay: fully supported.** The `draw` array (via `cf.image.draw` or the binding's `.draw()` method) lets you composite multiple images with positioning (`top`, `left`, `bottom`, `right`), opacity, repeat (tiling for watermarks), fit, gravity, and rotation.

**Text overlay: not directly supported.** Cloudflare has no built-in text rendering. The documented workaround is a two-step approach:
1. Deploy a separate Worker (or route) that converts text to a PNG image (using a library like `workers-og` / Satori).
2. Use that rendered text-image as an overlay URL in the `draw` array.

This means generating a full OG card with text + background image requires either the two-Worker pipeline above, or doing the entire composition in-Worker with a library.

### 4. npm Packages for Image Manipulation in Workers

| Package | What it does | Workers-compatible? |
|---|---|---|
| **[workers-og](https://github.com/kvnang/workers-og)** | OG image generation using Satori (HTML/CSS to SVG to PNG). Browser-less, edge-native. Inspired by `@vercel/og`. | Yes -- designed for Workers |
| **[@cf-wasm/photon](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/photon)** | Full image processing (resize, crop, watermark, filters) via Photon (Rust compiled to WASM). | Yes |
| **[@cf-wasm/png](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/png)** | PNG encode/decode in WASM. | Yes |
| **[@cf-wasm/og](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/og)** | OG image generation variant for specific frameworks. | Yes (workerd runtime) |
| **Sharp** | Popular Node.js image library. | **No** -- uses native modules, incompatible with Workers. |

---

## Practical Recommendation: Generating a 1200x630 OG Image

For your nasapicture.com project, you have two viable approaches:

### Option A: `workers-og` (Simplest)

Use `workers-og` to render an OG card entirely from HTML/CSS markup. You would define a JSX-like template with the APOD title, date, and a background image (the APOD photo fetched as a base64 data URL or referenced by URL). Satori renders it to SVG, then to PNG -- all in-Worker, no browser needed.

- **Pros:** Single Worker, declarative layout with familiar HTML/CSS syntax, purpose-built for this exact use case, lightweight.
- **Cons:** Satori supports a subset of CSS (flexbox only, no grid). Custom fonts require embedding font files as ArrayBuffers, which adds to bundle size. Background images from external URLs may add latency on cold starts.
- **Cost:** Falls within the free Workers tier. No Cloudflare Images billing since you are generating the image yourself (not using the Images API).

### Option B: Cloudflare Images Binding + `workers-og` for Text

Use the Images binding to resize/crop the APOD photo to 1200x630, then `.draw()` a text overlay (rendered separately as a PNG via `workers-og` or a pre-made logo PNG).

- **Pros:** Cloudflare handles the heavy image processing (resize, crop, format conversion) natively and efficiently. You get high-quality resizing with proper `fit`/`gravity` options.
- **Cons:** Two-step pipeline. Each call to the Images binding counts as a transformation (billable after 5,000/month). More moving parts.
- **Cost:** Free up to 5,000 unique transformations/month, then $0.50/1,000. For an OG image that changes once per day, you would use roughly 1 unique transformation per day (about 30/month) -- well within free tier.

### Recommended approach

**Option A (`workers-og`) is the right fit for this project.** You only need one OG image per day (the daily APOD), the layout is simple (photo + title + branding), and it avoids adding the Images binding dependency. Cache the result aggressively (24h TTL keyed to the date) and you get a single cold generation per day with zero ongoing cost.

If you later need higher-fidelity image processing (smart cropping with face detection, format negotiation, etc.), Option B with the Images binding is the upgrade path, and it stays free at your volume.

---

- [Bind to Workers API -- Cloudflare Images docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/bindings/)
- [Draw overlays and watermarks -- Cloudflare Images docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/draw-overlays/)
- [Pricing -- Cloudflare Images docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/pricing/)
- [Merging Images and Image Resizing -- Cloudflare Blog](https://blog.cloudflare.com/merging-images-and-image-resizing/)
- [workers-og on GitHub](https://github.com/kvnang/workers-og)
- [Generate YouTube thumbnails with Workers -- Cloudflare tutorial](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/tutorials/generate-youtube-thumbnails-with-workers-and-images/)
- [@cf-wasm/photon on npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/photon)
```

```text
I found comprehensive information about Cloudflare Image Resizing with Workers and the `cf` object options. Here's what the search results reveal:

## Cloudflare Image Resizing with Workers - `cf` Object Options

In your Worker, you can add image resizing options using the `cf` object when fetching an image.

### Key Image Options Available

The `cf` object supports various transformation options within the `image` property:

**Dimensions & Fit:**
- width, height, and fit properties (like 'scale-down')

**Image Adjustments:**
- exposure: A value of 1.0 equals no change, a value of 0.5 darkens the image, and a value of 2.0 lightens the image
- brightness: A value of 1.0 equals no change, a value of 0.5 equals half brightness, and a value of 2.0 equals twice as bright
- rotate: Shows the degrees (90, 180, or 270) to rotate the image
- sharpen: The sharpening filter strength to be applied to the image

**Format & Quality:**
- quality: Specifies quality for images in JPEG, WebP, and AVIF formats
- json: Instead of generating an image, outputs information about the image in JSON format, containing data such as image size and MIME type

**Effects:**
- blur: Blur radius between 1 (slight blur) and 250 (maximum)
- border: Adds a border around the image after resizing

**Cropping:**
- gravity: Specifies how an image should be cropped, with options including auto, face, sides (left, right, top, bottom), and relative coordinates

The documentation referenced is current as of 2025-2026 based on the search results.
```

```text
Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of Cloudflare Images vs Image Resizing:

## Product Merger

Cloudflare merged their Images and Image Resizing products in November 2023, creating a single product with unified pricing. All Image Resizing features are now available as part of the Cloudflare Images product.

## Current Pricing Structure

The merged Cloudflare Images product uses pricing of $0.50/1,000 transforms, $5/100K stored, and $1/100K delivered.

### Pricing Breakdown:

1. **Transformations**: $0.50 per 1,000 unique transformations. Each unique transformation is billed only once per calendar month.

2. **Storage & Delivery**: Storage and delivery fees only apply if you store images in Cloudflare's own Images bucket. If you transform images from an external origin (your server, R2, S3), you pay only the transformation fee.

## Key Advantages Over Legacy Image Resizing

The merger addressed a major pricing issue. Legacy Image Resizing was billed by the number of uncached requests to transform an image. However, caching behavior is often unpredictable, and you can't guarantee how long a given image stays cached, meaning you can't reliably predict costs. The new model provides more predictable pricing based on unique transformations rather than cache-dependent requests.

## Availability

Cloudflare Images is available on both Free and Paid plans. By default, all users have access to the Images Free plan, which includes limited usage of the transformations feature to optimize images in remote sources.
```

```text
Based on the search results, here's what I found about Cloudflare Workers image manipulation, compositing, text overlays, and OG image generation:

## Key Capabilities

Cloudflare Workers allows you to transform, overlay, and encode images within the Cloudflare Developer Platform ecosystem. The Images binding in Workers makes the development experience more accessible and intuitive by simplifying how you connect the Images API to your Worker and providing more fine-grained control over how images are optimized.

## Image Compositing and Overlays

You can draw additional images on top of a resized image, with transparency and blending effects, enabling adding of watermarks, logos, signatures, vignettes, and other effects to resized images. When interacting with Images through a binding, the Images API supports a `.draw()` method with options for opacity, repeat, top, left, bottom, and right.

## Text Overlay Implementation

For adding text overlays to images, the approach involves converting text to images first. You can create a Worker that transforms text to image, which can then be used as an overlay on a background image. You can resize images and use the draw option to add overlay text using the deployed URL of your text-to-image Worker.

## OG Image Generation

An `og:image` (social card) generator can be fast, browser-less, and capable of running on the edge, designed to be used with Cloudflare Workers.

The search results show that Cloudflare Workers with the Images binding provides a complete solution for dynamic image manipulation, including compositing, overlays, and OG image generation at the edge.
```

```text
Based on the search results, here are the key npm packages and solutions for image manipulation and OG image generation in Cloudflare Workers:

## OG Image Generation

**workers-og** is an og:image (social card) generator that is fast, browser-less, and capable of running on the edge, designed to be used with Cloudflare Workers. The workers-og library uses Satori under the hood for rendering, which means it's converting your HTML and CSS into an image without needing a browser.

For SvelteKit on Cloudflare, you can use **@cf-wasm/og/workerd**.

## Image Manipulation Packages

For general image manipulation, **Photon** is a high-performance Rust image processing library which compiles to WebAssembly and offers a variety of functions for resizing, transforming, watermarking, and more. There's an npm package available called **@cf-wasm/photon**.

**@cf-wasm/png** is a simple wasm png encoder/decoder module for Cloudflare Workers, and it can be used in combination with Photon for image processing tasks.

**upng.js** can convert raw byte data to and from .png files, allowing modification of raw pixel values.

## Runtime Compatibility

Sharp does not work on Cloudflare Workers as it uses Node.js APIs, which are not yet supported in Cloudflare's workerd runtime. The recommended approach is using WebAssembly-based libraries like Photon instead.
```

```text
# Draw overlays and watermarks
You can draw additional images on top of a resized image, with transparency and blending effects. This enables adding of watermarks, logos, signatures, vignettes, and other effects to resized images.
This feature is available only in [Workers](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/). To draw overlay images, add an array of drawing commands to options of `fetch()` requests. The drawing options are nested in `options.cf.image.draw`, like in the following example:
JavaScript
```
fetch(imageURL, {  cf: {    image: {      width: 800,      height: 600,      draw: [        {          url: "https://example.com/branding/logo.png", // draw this image          bottom: 5, // 5 pixels from the bottom edge          right: 5, // 5 pixels from the right edge          fit: "contain", // make it fit within 100x50 area          width: 100,          height: 50,          opacity: 0.8, // 20% transparent        },      ],    },  },});
```
*JavaScript*
## Draw options
[](#draw-options)
The `draw` property is an array. Overlays are drawn in the order they appear in the array (the last array entry is the topmost layer). Each item in the `draw` array is an object, which can have the following properties:
-   `url`
    -   Absolute URL of the image file to use for the drawing. It can be any of the supported file formats. For drawing watermarks or non-rectangular overlays, Cloudflare recommends that you use PNG or WebP images.
-   `width` and `height`
    -   Maximum size of the overlay image, in pixels. It must be an integer.
-   `fit` and `gravity`
    -   Affects interpretation of `width` and `height`. Same as [for the main image](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/#fetch-options).
-   `opacity`
    -   Floating-point number between `0` (transparent) and `1` (opaque). For example, `opacity: 0.5` makes overlay semitransparent.
-   `repeat`
    -   If set to `true`, the overlay image will be tiled to cover the entire area. This is useful for stock-photo-like watermarks.
    -   If set to `"x"`, the overlay image will be tiled horizontally only (form a line).
    -   If set to `"y"`, the overlay image will be tiled vertically only (form a line).
-   `top`, `left`, `bottom`, `right`
    -   Position of the overlay image relative to a given edge. Each property is an offset in pixels. `0` aligns exactly to the edge. For example, `left: 10` positions left side of the overlay 10 pixels from the left edge of the image it is drawn over. `bottom: 0` aligns bottom of the overlay with bottom of the background image.
        Setting both `left` and `right`, or both `top` and `bottom` is an error.
        If no position is specified, the image will be centered.
-   `background`
    -   Background color to add underneath the overlay image. Same as [for the main image](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/#fetch-options).
-   `rotate`
    -   Number of degrees to rotate the overlay image by. Same as [for the main image](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/#fetch-options).
## Draw using the Images binding
[](#draw-using-the-images-binding)
When [interacting with Images through a binding](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/bindings/), the Images API supports a `.draw()` method.
The accepted options for the overlaid image are `opacity`, `repeat`, `top`, `left`, `bottom`, and `right`.
JavaScript
```
// Fetch image and watermarkconst img = await fetch("https://example.com/image.png");const watermark = await fetch("https://example.com/watermark.png");
const response = (  await env.IMAGES.input(img.body)    .transform({ width: 1024 })    .draw(watermark.body, { opacity: 0.25, repeat: true })    .output({ format: "image/avif" })).response();
return response;
```
*JavaScript*
To apply [parameters](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/) to the overlaid image, you can pass a child `.transform()` function inside the `.draw()` request.
In the example below, the watermark is manipulated with `rotate` and `width` before being drawn over the base image with the `opacity` and `rotate` options.
JavaScript
```
// Fetch image and watermarkconst response = (  await env.IMAGES.input(img.body)    .transform({ width: 1024 })    .draw(watermark.body, { opacity: 0.25, repeat: true })    .output({ format: "image/avif" })).response();
```
*JavaScript*
## Examples
[](#examples)
### Stock Photo Watermark
[](#stock-photo-watermark)
JavaScript
```
image: {  draw: [    {      url: 'https://example.com/watermark.png',      repeat: true, // Tiled over entire image      opacity: 0.2, // and subtly blended    },  ],}
```
*JavaScript*
### Signature
[](#signature)
JavaScript
```
image: {  draw: [    {      url: 'https://example.com/by-me.png', // Predefined logo/signature      bottom: 5, // Positioned near bottom right corner      right: 5,    },  ],}
```
*JavaScript*
### Centered icon
[](#centered-icon)
JavaScript
```
image: {  draw: [    {      url: 'https://example.com/play-button.png',      // Center position is the default    },  ],}
```
*JavaScript*
### Combined
[](#combined)
Multiple operations can be combined in one image:
JavaScript
```
image: {  draw: [    { url: 'https://example.com/watermark.png', repeat: true, opacity: 0.2 },    { url: 'https://example.com/play-button.png' },    { url: 'https://example.com/by-me.png', bottom: 5, right: 5 },  ],}
```
*JavaScript*
```

```text
# Pricing
By default, all users are on the Images Free plan. The Free plan includes access to the transformations feature, which lets you optimize images stored outside of Images, like in R2.
The Paid plan allows transformations, as well as access to storage in Images.
Pricing is dependent on which features you use. The table below shows which metrics are used for each use case.
| Use case | Metrics | Availability |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Optimize images stored outside of Images | Images Transformed | Free and Paid plans |
| Optimized images that are stored in Cloudflare Images | Images Stored, Images Delivered | Only Paid plans |
## Images Free
[](#images-free)
On the Free plan, you can request up to 5,000 unique transformations each month for free.
Once you exceed 5,000 unique transformations:
-   Existing transformations in cache will continue to be served as expected.
-   New transformations will return a `9422` error. If your source image is from the same domain where the transformation is served, then you can use the [`onerror` parameter](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-url/#onerror) to redirect to the original image.
-   You will not be charged for exceeding the limits in the Free plan.
To request more than 5,000 unique transformations each month, you can purchase an Images Paid plan.
## Images Paid
[](#images-paid)
When you purchase an Images Paid plan, you can choose your own storage or add storage in Images.
| Metric | Pricing |
| --- | --- |
| Images Transformed | First 5,000 unique transformations included + $0.50 / 1,000 unique transformations / month |
| Images Stored | $5 / 100,000 images stored / month |
| Images Delivered | $1 / 100,000 images delivered / month |
If you optimize an image stored outside of Images, then you will be billed only for Images Transformed.
Alternatively, Images Stored and Images Delivered apply only to images that are stored in your Images bucket. When you optimize an image that is stored in Images, then this counts toward Images Delivered — not Images Transformed.
## Metrics
[](#metrics)
### Images Transformed
[](#images-transformed)
A unique transformation is a request to transform an original image based on a set of [supported parameters](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-url/#options). This metric is used only when optimizing images that are stored outside of Images. When using the [Images binding](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/bindings/) in Workers, every call to the binding counts as a transformation, regardless of whether the image or parameters are unique.
For example, if you transform `thumbnail.jpg` as 100x100, then this counts as one unique transformation. If you transform the same `thumbnail.jpg` as 200x200, then this counts as a separate unique transformation.
You are billed on the number of unique transformations that are requested within each calendar month. Repeat requests for the same transformation within the same month are counted only once for that month.
The `format` parameter counts as only one billable transformation, even if multiple copies of an image are served. In other words, if `width=100,format=auto/thumbnail.jpg` is served to some users as AVIF and to others as WebP, then this counts as one unique transformation instead of two.
#### Example #1
[](#example-1)
If you serve 2,000 remote images in five different sizes each month, then this results in 10,000 unique transformations. Your estimated cost for the month would be:
|  | Usage | Included | Billable quantity | Price |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Transformations | 10,000 unique transformations [1](#user-content-fn-5) | 5,000 | 5,000 | $2.50 [2](#user-content-fn-6) |
#### Example #2
[](#example-2)
If you use [R2](https://developers.cloudflare.com/r2/) for storage then your estimated monthly costs will be the sum of your monthly Images costs and monthly [R2 costs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/r2/pricing/#storage-usage).
For example, if you upload 5,000 images to R2 with an average size of 5 MB, and serve 2,000 of those images in five different sizes, then your estimated cost for the month would be:
|  | Usage | Included | Billable quantity | Price |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Storage | 25 GB [3](#user-content-fn-1) | 10 GB | 15 GB | $0.22 [4](#user-content-fn-7) |
| Class A operations | 5,000 writes [5](#user-content-fn-2) | 1 million | 0 | $0.00 [6](#user-content-fn-8) |
| Class B operations | 10,000 reads [7](#user-content-fn-3) | 10 million | 0 | $0.00 [8](#user-content-fn-9) |
| Transformations | 10,000 unique transformations [9](#user-content-fn-4) | 5,000 | 5,000 | $2.50 [10](#user-content-fn-10) |
| **Total** |  |  |  | **$2.72** |
### Images Stored
[](#images-stored)
Storage in Images is available only with an Images Paid plan. You can purchase storage in increments of $5 for every 100,000 images stored per month.
You can create predefined variants to specify how an image should be resized, such as `thumbnail` as 100x100 and `hero` as 1600x500.
Only uploaded images count toward Images Stored; defining variants will not impact your storage limit.
### Images Delivered
[](#images-delivered)
For images that are stored in Images, you will incur $1 for every 100,000 images delivered per month. This metric does not include transformed images that are stored in remote sources.
Every image requested by the browser counts as one billable request.
#### Example
[](#example)
A retail website has a product page that uses Images to serve 10 images. If the page was visited 10,000 times this month, then this results in 100,000 images delivered — or $1.00 in billable usage.
## Footnotes
[](#footnote-label)
1.  2,000 original images × 5 sizes [↩](#user-content-fnref-5)
2.  (5,000 transformations / 1,000) × $0.50 [↩](#user-content-fnref-6)
3.  5,000 objects × 5 MB per object [↩](#user-content-fnref-1)
4.  15 GB × $0.015 / GB-month [↩](#user-content-fnref-7)
5.  5,000 objects × 1 write per object [↩](#user-content-fnref-2)
6.  0 × $4.50 / million requests [↩](#user-content-fnref-8)
7.  2,000 objects × 5 reads per object [↩](#user-content-fnref-3)
8.  0 × $0.36 / million requests [↩](#user-content-fnref-9)
9.  2,000 original images × 5 sizes [↩](#user-content-fnref-4)
10.  (5,000 transformations / 1,000) × $0.50 [↩](#user-content-fnref-10)
```

```text
# Bind to Workers API
A [binding](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/bindings/) connects your [Worker](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/) to external resources on the Developer Platform, like [Images](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/), [R2 buckets](https://developers.cloudflare.com/r2/buckets/), or [KV Namespaces](https://developers.cloudflare.com/kv/concepts/kv-namespaces/).
You can bind the Images API to your Worker to transform, resize, and encode images without requiring them to be accessible through a URL.
For example, when you allow Workers to interact with Images, you can:
-   Transform an image, then upload the output image directly into R2 without serving to the browser.
-   Optimize an image stored in R2 by passing the blob of bytes representing the image, instead of fetching the public URL for the image.
-   Resize an image, overlay the output over a second image as a watermark, then resize this output into a final result.
Bindings can be configured in the Cloudflare dashboard for your Worker or in the Wrangler configuration file in your project's directory.
Billing
Every call to the Images binding counts as one unique transformation. Refer to [Images pricing](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/pricing/) for more information about transformation billing.
## Setup
[](#setup)
The Images binding is enabled on a per-Worker basis.
You can define variables in the Wrangler configuration file of your Worker project's directory. These variables are bound to external resources at runtime, and you can then interact with them through this variable.
To bind Images to your Worker, add the following to the end of your Wrangler configuration file:
-   [wrangler.jsonc](#tab-panel-4809)
-   [wrangler.toml](#tab-panel-4810)
```
{  "images": {    "binding": "IMAGES", // i.e. available in your Worker on env.IMAGES  },}
```
**
```
[images]binding = "IMAGES"
```
**
Within your Worker code, you can interact with this binding by using `env.IMAGES.input()` to build an object that can manipulate the image (passed as a `ReadableStream`).
## Methods
[](#methods)
### `.transform()`
[](#transform)
-   Defines how an image should be optimized and manipulated through [parameters](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/#fetch-options) such as `width`, `height`, and `blur`.
### `.draw()`
[](#draw)
-   Allows [drawing an image](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/draw-overlays/) over another image.
-   The drawn image can be a stream, or another image returned from `.input()` that has been manipulated.
-   The overlaid image can be manipulated using `opacity`, `repeat`, `top`, `left`, `bottom`, and `right`. To apply other parameters, you can pass a child `.transform()` function inside this method.
For example, to draw a resized watermark on an image:
-   [JavaScript](#tab-panel-4811)
-   [TypeScript](#tab-panel-4812)
JavaScript
```
// Fetch the watermark from Workers Assets, R2, KV etcconst watermark = getWatermarkStream();
// Fetch the main imageconst image = getImageStream();
const response = (  await env.IMAGES.input(image)    .draw(env.IMAGES.input(watermark).transform({ width: 32, height: 32 }), {      bottom: 32,      right: 32,    })    .output({ format: "image/avif" })).response();
return response;
```
*JavaScript*
TypeScript
```
// Fetch the watermark from Workers Assets, R2, KV etcconst watermark: ReadableStream = getWatermarkStream();
// Fetch the main imageconst image: ReadableStream = getImageStream();
const response = (  await env.IMAGES.input(image)    .draw(env.IMAGES.input(watermark).transform({ width: 32, height: 32 }), {      bottom: 32,      right: 32,    })    .output({ format: "image/avif" })).response();
return response;
```
*TypeScript*
### `.output()`
[](#output)
-   You must define [a supported format](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/#supported-output-formats) such as AVIF, WebP, or JPEG for the [transformed image](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/).
-   This is required since there is no default format to fallback to.
-   [Image quality](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-url/#quality) can be altered by specifying `quality` on a 1-100 scale.
-   [Animation preservation](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-url/#anim) can be controlled with the `anim` parameter. Set `anim: false` to reduce animations to still images.
For example, to rotate, resize, and blur an image, then output the image as AVIF:
-   [JavaScript](#tab-panel-4813)
-   [TypeScript](#tab-panel-4814)
JavaScript
```
const info = await env.IMAGES.info(stream);// Stream contains a valid image, and width/height is available on the info object
// You can determine the format based on the use caseconst outputFormat = "image/avif";
const response = (  await env.IMAGES.input(stream)    .transform({ rotate: 90 })    .transform({ width: 128 })    .transform({ blur: 20 })    .output({ format: outputFormat })).response();
return response;
```
*JavaScript*
TypeScript
```
const info = await env.IMAGES.info(stream);// Stream contains a valid image, and width/height is available on the info object
// You can determine the format based on the use caseconst outputFormat = "image/avif";
const response = (  await env.IMAGES.input(stream)    .transform({ rotate: 90 })    .transform({ width: 128 })    .transform({ blur: 20 })    .output({ format: outputFormat })).response();
return response;
```
*TypeScript*
### `.info()`
[](#info)
-   Outputs information about the image, such as `format`, `fileSize`, `width`, and `height`.
Note
Responses from the Images binding are not automatically cached. Workers lets you interact directly with the [Cache API](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/runtime-apis/cache/) to customize cache behavior. You can implement logic in your script to store transformations in Cloudflare's cache.
## Interact with your Images binding locally
[](#interact-with-your-images-binding-locally)
The Images API can be used in local development through [Wrangler](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/wrangler/install-and-update/), the command-line interface for Workers. Using the Images binding in local development will not incur usage charges.
Wrangler supports two different versions of the Images API:
-   A high-fidelity version that supports all features that are available through the Images API. This is the same version that Cloudflare runs globally in production.
-   A low-fidelity offline version that supports only a subset of features, such as resizing and rotation.
To test the low-fidelity version of Images, you can run `wrangler dev`:
```
npx wrangler dev
```
**
Currently, this version supports only `width`, `height`, `rotate`, and `format`.
To test the high-fidelity remote version of Images, you can use the `--remote` flag:
```
npx wrangler dev --remote
```
**
When testing with the [Workers Vitest integration](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/testing/vitest-integration/), the low-fidelity offline version is used by default, to avoid hitting the Cloudflare API in tests.
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 04:28:15Z)**_

Here is a summary of the findings.

---

## Cloudflare Image Tools -- Research Summary

### 1. Cloudflare Image Resizing / Transformations

**What it does:** Resize, crop, rotate, adjust quality, blur, sharpen, and change format of images on the fly. Two ways to use it from a Worker:

- **`cf.image` on `fetch()` calls** -- pass options like `width`, `height`, `fit`, `gravity`, `quality`, `format`, `draw` (overlays) directly in the fetch options object.
- **Images binding (`env.IMAGES`)** -- a newer, more flexible API. You add `[images] binding = "IMAGES"` to `wrangler.toml`, then chain `.input()`, `.transform()`, `.draw()`, `.output()` calls. This works with raw streams (no public URL required), so you can pipe bytes from R2, KV, or another fetch.

**Pricing:**
- **Free plan: 5,000 unique transformations per month at no cost.** Beyond that, new transformations return a 9422 error (cached ones keep working).
- **Paid plan:** First 5,000 included, then **$0.50 per 1,000 unique transformations/month.** A "unique transformation" = one original image + one set of parameters. The same combo requested again in the same month is not re-billed. The `format=auto` parameter (serving AVIF vs WebP to different clients) counts as only one transformation.
- Important: with the Images binding, **every call counts as a transformation** regardless of uniqueness.

**Available on free plans?** Yes, with the 5,000/month cap. No credit card required to start.

### 2. Cloudflare Images (Storage Product) vs Image Resizing

These were merged into a single product in late 2023. The distinction now is:

| Feature | Transformations (free + paid) | Images Storage (paid only) |
|---|---|---|
| Transform remote images (your origin, R2, etc.) | Yes | N/A |
| Store images in CF's managed bucket | No | Yes ($5/100K stored/mo) |
| Serve stored images | N/A | Yes ($1/100K delivered/mo) |
| Predefined variants (thumbnail, hero, etc.) | N/A | Yes |

For your use case (proxying NASA APOD), you would only use the **Transformations** feature against a remote origin. You would not need the storage product.

### 3. Image Compositing (Text Overlay, Logo Overlay)

**Image-over-image overlay: fully supported.** The `draw` array (via `cf.image.draw` or the binding's `.draw()` method) lets you composite multiple images with positioning (`top`, `left`, `bottom`, `right`), opacity, repeat (tiling for watermarks), fit, gravity, and rotation.

**Text overlay: not directly supported.** Cloudflare has no built-in text rendering. The documented workaround is a two-step approach:
1. Deploy a separate Worker (or route) that converts text to a PNG image (using a library like `workers-og` / Satori).
2. Use that rendered text-image as an overlay URL in the `draw` array.

This means generating a full OG card with text + background image requires either the two-Worker pipeline above, or doing the entire composition in-Worker with a library.

### 4. npm Packages for Image Manipulation in Workers

| Package | What it does | Workers-compatible? |
|---|---|---|
| **[workers-og](https://github.com/kvnang/workers-og)** | OG image generation using Satori (HTML/CSS to SVG to PNG). Browser-less, edge-native. Inspired by `@vercel/og`. | Yes -- designed for Workers |
| **[@cf-wasm/photon](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/photon)** | Full image processing (resize, crop, watermark, filters) via Photon (Rust compiled to WASM). | Yes |
| **[@cf-wasm/png](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/png)** | PNG encode/decode in WASM. | Yes |
| **[@cf-wasm/og](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/og)** | OG image generation variant for specific frameworks. | Yes (workerd runtime) |
| **Sharp** | Popular Node.js image library. | **No** -- uses native modules, incompatible with Workers. |

---

## Practical Recommendation: Generating a 1200x630 OG Image

For your nasapicture.com project, you have two viable approaches:

### Option A: `workers-og` (Simplest)

Use `workers-og` to render an OG card entirely from HTML/CSS markup. You would define a JSX-like template with the APOD title, date, and a background image (the APOD photo fetched as a base64 data URL or referenced by URL). Satori renders it to SVG, then to PNG -- all in-Worker, no browser needed.

- **Pros:** Single Worker, declarative layout with familiar HTML/CSS syntax, purpose-built for this exact use case, lightweight.
- **Cons:** Satori supports a subset of CSS (flexbox only, no grid). Custom fonts require embedding font files as ArrayBuffers, which adds to bundle size. Background images from external URLs may add latency on cold starts.
- **Cost:** Falls within the free Workers tier. No Cloudflare Images billing since you are generating the image yourself (not using the Images API).

### Option B: Cloudflare Images Binding + `workers-og` for Text

Use the Images binding to resize/crop the APOD photo to 1200x630, then `.draw()` a text overlay (rendered separately as a PNG via `workers-og` or a pre-made logo PNG).

- **Pros:** Cloudflare handles the heavy image processing (resize, crop, format conversion) natively and efficiently. You get high-quality resizing with proper `fit`/`gravity` options.
- **Cons:** Two-step pipeline. Each call to the Images binding counts as a transformation (billable after 5,000/month). More moving parts.
- **Cost:** Free up to 5,000 unique transformations/month, then $0.50/1,000. For an OG image that changes once per day, you would use roughly 1 unique transformation per day (about 30/month) -- well within free tier.

### Recommended approach

**Option A (`workers-og`) is the right fit for this project.** You only need one OG image per day (the daily APOD), the layout is simple (photo + title + branding), and it avoids adding the Images binding dependency. Cache the result aggressively (24h TTL keyed to the date) and you get a single cold generation per day with zero ongoing cost.

If you later need higher-fidelity image processing (smart cropping with face detection, format negotiation, etc.), Option B with the Images binding is the upgrade path, and it stays free at your volume.

---

Sources:
- [Transform via Workers -- Cloudflare Images docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/transform-via-workers/)
- [Bind to Workers API -- Cloudflare Images docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/bindings/)
- [Draw overlays and watermarks -- Cloudflare Images docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/draw-overlays/)
- [Pricing -- Cloudflare Images docs](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/pricing/)
- [Merging Images and Image Resizing -- Cloudflare Blog](https://blog.cloudflare.com/merging-images-and-image-resizing/)
- [workers-og on GitHub](https://github.com/kvnang/workers-og)
- [Generate YouTube thumbnails with Workers -- Cloudflare tutorial](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/tutorials/generate-youtube-thumbnails-with-workers-and-images/)

```md
---
type: note
status: inbox
created: 2026-03-31
---

# Generate a daily OG image

Generate a new Open Graph image every day, resized and cropped to the optimal 1200x630px dimensions. Adding a text overlay with the logo would be a bonus.

## Context

The current OG image points to `https://api.nasapicture.com/image.jpg`, which serves the raw APOD at whatever dimensions NASA provides (often square or portrait). Social platforms expect 1200x630px for optimal previews.

## Recommended approach: `workers-og`

[`workers-og`](https://github.com/kvnang/workers-og) renders HTML/CSS to PNG entirely inside a Worker (Satori → SVG → PNG, no browser needed). This is purpose-built for OG image generation at the edge.

**How it would work:**
1. Add a `/og` route to the existing Worker (or a separate one)
2. Fetch today's APOD metadata from `/info` (title, date)
3. Render an HTML template with the APOD photo as background, title text, and nasapicture.com branding
4. Cache the result aggressively (24h TTL keyed to date) — one cold generation per day
5. Update the `og:image` meta tag to point to the new `/og` route

**Pros:** Single Worker, zero external dependencies, familiar HTML/CSS for layout, no billing (stays within free Workers tier since we're generating the image ourselves, not using Cloudflare's Images API).

**Cons:** Satori supports flexbox only (no CSS grid). Custom fonts need to be embedded as ArrayBuffers (adds to bundle size). Subset of CSS supported.

## Alternative: Cloudflare Images binding

Use the Images binding (`env.IMAGES`) to resize/crop the APOD to 1200x630 natively, then `.draw()` a pre-rendered text overlay PNG on top.

- Requires `[images] binding = "IMAGES"` in `wrangler.toml`
- Free tier: 5,000 unique transformations/month (we'd use ~30/month — well within limits)
- Higher-fidelity resizing with smart crop/gravity options
- More moving parts (two-step: resize + composite)

This is the upgrade path if `workers-og` quality isn't sufficient or if we need smarter cropping (e.g. face/subject detection).

## Not viable

- **Sharp** — uses native Node.js modules, incompatible with Workers
- **Canvas APIs** — no browser context in Workers

## Useful links

- [workers-og](https://github.com/kvnang/workers-og)
- [@cf-wasm/photon](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@cf-wasm/photon) — WASM image processing if more control is needed
- [Cloudflare Images: draw overlays](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/transform-images/draw-overlays/)
- [Cloudflare Images pricing](https://developers.cloudflare.com/images/pricing/) — free up to 5,000 transformations/month

```

```text
The file /Users/grant/code/nasa-apod/board/planning/NOTE-0008_generate-daily-og-image.md has been updated successfully.
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 04:28:43Z)**_

Updated NOTE-0008 with research. The short version: `workers-og` is the best fit — it renders HTML/CSS to PNG entirely inside a Worker, no external services or billing. Add a `/og` route, cache it daily, done. Cloudflare Images binding is the upgrade path if you need higher-quality resizing later.

_**User (2026-04-01 04:35:13Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 04:35:23Z)**_



It's straightforward conceptually, but there are a few things that could get fiddly:

- **Font embedding** — Satori needs font files as `ArrayBuffer`s bundled into the Worker. You're using Cormorant Garamond, Outfit, and JetBrains Mono — each weight adds to bundle size, and Workers have a 10MB compressed limit
- **Background image** — fetching the APOD and embedding it in the template adds latency on the cold generation. The image also needs to look good cropped to 1200x630 regardless of the original aspect ratio — some APODs are wide panoramas, others are nearly square
- **Satori CSS subset** — no grid, limited `background-image` support, no `object-fit`. Layouts that look trivial in real CSS sometimes need workarounds in Satori
- **Cache invalidation** — keying on date is simple, but timezone edge cases (the APOD rolls over on Eastern time) need handling

Still very doable, but it's probably a half-day of work, not a 20-minute thing.

Good call — a pre-made logo image sidesteps the font bundling entirely. The template becomes just the APOD photo cropped to 1200x630 with a small logo PNG composited on top. That really does simplify it significantly.

## Steps

### 1. Review Board State

Parse the pre-loaded board data above. Identify:
- All items with `status: backlog` or `status: current` (candidates for completion)
- Epics and their child tasks (via `parent` field)
- Blocked items and their blockers
- Items with terminal status (`done`, `processed`) that should be archived

### 2. Gather Codebase Context

- Run `git log --oneline` for recent commits (last 30) to understand what work has been done
- Only Read full file bodies for tasks whose status is `backlog` or `current` AND whose title suggests overlap with recent commits
- Use the context budget escalation (Glob → Grep → Read with limit → Full read) for any additional lookups

### 3. Detect Completed Work

For each task with `status: backlog` or `status: current`, check if the work described has already been done:

- Look at recent commits for evidence the work was completed
- Check the codebase for the feature/fix the task describes
- If the work is done, flag it for status update to `done`

### 4. Detect New Work Needed

Scan for things that should become tasks but aren't tracked yet:

- Check recent commits for TODOs or FIXMEs introduced in code
- Look at open epics — are there obvious next steps not yet captured as tasks?
- Check if any `blocked_by` dependencies have been resolved (unblocking tasks)

### 5. Update Epic Progress

For each active epic:

- Check if all child tasks are `done` — if so, flag the epic as `done`
- Check if any child tasks are `current` — if so, the epic should be `current`
- Update the task list in the epic body if tasks have been added/removed

### 6. Check for Stale Items

- Tasks marked `current` but with no related commits in the last 30 days
- Epics in `planning` with no child tasks
- Resources stuck in `inbox` or `processing`

### 7. Present Changes

Present ALL proposed changes as a single summary before making any edits:

```
## Board Update Proposal

### Status Changes
- TASK-0003: backlog -> done (cache-decoded-image: implemented in commit a1b2c3d)
- EPIC-0001: planning -> current (has active child tasks)

### New Tasks Suggested
- "Fix FIXME in preprocessing.rs line 42" (found in recent commit)

### Ready to Archive
- TASK-0003: done
- NOTE-0002: processed

### Stale Items
- TASK-0007: marked current but no activity in 45 days

### Unblocked
- TASK-0005: blocker TASK-0003 is now done
```

**Avoid redundant invocations** — if this skill was already invoked earlier in the session and the board has not been modified since, reuse the previous board data instead of invoking again.

Ask the user to approve, reject, or cherry-pick which changes to apply. Then execute only the approved changes:

- **Status updates**: Use `/board:board-edit` to update status fields
- **New tasks**: Invoke `/board:board-add` for each approved new task
- **Archiving**: Run `bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/archive-batch.sh ./board <file1> <file2> ...` directly for approved items

# Add Board Item

Create a new item in the `board/` project management system. The system spec is bundled with the plugin at `/Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/spec/board-system-spec.md`.

## Existing Board Items

Pre-loaded board data for duplicate detection:

filepath	type	status	priority	blocked_by	parent	title
./board/tasks/TASK-0003_nasa-data-insignia.md	task	todo	-	-	-	Apply for NASA Data Insignia
./board/tasks/TASK-0004_qa-cache-logging-deploy.md	task	todo	-	-	-	QA cache and logging before deploy
./board/planning/NOTE-0001_domain-strategy.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Domain strategy for nasapicture.com
./board/planning/NOTE-0002_landing-page.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Landing page for nasapicture.com
./board/planning/NOTE-0003_landing-page-critique.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Landing page design critique (gallery revision)
./board/planning/NOTE-0005_add-individual-metadata-routes.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Add individual metadata routes
./board/planning/NOTE-0006_add-why-is-this-needed-section.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Add a "Why Is This Needed" Section That Explains the APOD API
./board/planning/NOTE-0007_add-turn-off-the-lights-dim-mode.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Add a "Turn Off the Lights" Icon for Semi-Isolated Image Viewing
./board/planning/NOTE-0008_generate-daily-og-image.md	note	inbox	-	-	-	Generate a daily OG image

Before creating, check if a similar item already exists. If a close match is found, ask the user if they still want to create a new one.

## Scope Assessment

When the user requests a **TASK**, evaluate whether it is actually EPIC-sized before creating it. Look for these signals:

- Multiple distinct steps or deliverables in the description
- Multiple action verbs joined by "and" (e.g., "add auth and set up roles and integrate SSO")
- Work that spans different code areas or concerns
- Sub-steps that could be completed and shipped independently

**Quick test:** "Could someone meaningfully complete and ship part of this while the rest remains undone?" If yes → likely an EPIC.

If EPIC signals are present, suggest an EPIC + sub-TASKs with a concrete breakdown. For example:

> "This looks like it has multiple independent deliverables. Want me to create an EPIC with separate TASKs for each piece instead?"

**This is a soft suggestion only.** If the user says "just make it a task," comply without pushback.

This assessment only applies to TASK creation — do not second-guess EPICs, NOTEs, or other types.

## Arguments

The user provides the type and title: `/board:board-add task <title>`, `/board:board-add epic <title>`, etc.

If no type is given, ask the user what type of item to create.

## Determine Next ID

Run the ID assignment script:

```bash
bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/next-id.sh <TYPE> ./board
```

This scans all directories (including `board/archive/` and `.doc-ids`) and returns the next available 4-digit zero-padded ID.

## Create the File

Use the correct directory, frontmatter, and naming convention:

| Type | Directory | Filename Example |
|------|-----------|-----------------|

## Scope Assessment

When the user requests a **TASK**, evaluate whether it is actually EPIC-sized before creating it. Look for these signals:

- Multiple distinct steps or deliverables in the description
- Multiple action verbs joined by "and" (e.g., "add auth and set up roles and integrate SSO")
- Work that spans different code areas or concerns
- Sub-steps that could be completed and shipped independently

**Quick test:** "Could someone meaningfully complete and ship part of this while the rest remains undone?" If yes → likely an EPIC.

If EPIC signals are present, suggest an EPIC + sub-TASKs with a concrete breakdown. For example:

> "This looks like it has multiple independent deliverables. Want me to create an EPIC with separate TASKs for each piece instead?"

**This is a soft suggestion only.** If the user says "just make it a task," comply without pushback.

This assessment only applies to TASK creation — do not second-guess EPICs, NOTEs, or other types.

```md
---

# Auto-refresh landing page on APOD rollover

If a user leaves the landing page open, the image and caption should update automatically when NASA publishes the new APOD — no manual reload required.

## Considerations

- NASA publishes around midnight Eastern, but the exact time varies (sometimes delayed)
- Polling `/info` periodically and comparing the date to what's currently displayed is the simplest approach
- A reasonable interval might be every 15–30 minutes — frequent enough to catch the rollover without hammering the API (which is cached on the Worker side anyway)
- When a new date is detected: update the image `src`, caption title, and date, with a fade transition
- Should only poll when the tab is visible (`document.visibilityState`) to avoid wasting requests in background tabs

```


+<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
+<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

+```md
+---
+name: Next session QA plan
+description: QA the Cache API and dev logging changes before deploying to production
+type: project
+---
+
+Next session starts with QA before deploying. Key changes to verify:
+
+1. **Workers Cache API** (`caches.default`) replaced in-memory Map + cf fetch options — cache should persist across wrangler reloads locally
+2. **No date param** for "current" APOD requests — NASA handles "today" timezone logic
+3. **Per-request log buffer** — `log[]` array threaded through functions instead of module-level `logBuffer`
+4. **Dev logging format** — horizontal rule separator, then request summary, then indented cache lines, then wrangler's own line
+
+**Why:** Previous in-memory cache was wiped on every wrangler reload, burning through DEMO_KEY rate limits (30 req/hr). The UTC date bug caused 404s from NASA after midnight UTC but before midnight Eastern.
+
+**How to apply:** Test all routes (`/`, `/sd`, `/info`, `/about`) — verify cache HITs on second request, verify logs are clean, then deploy.
+
